generated_comment: I may be old-fashioned here, but from a business point of view these extra leave policies need a fair discussion. If one team member can be out for period leave and later long maternity leave, the workload and project pressure automatically shift to the rest of the team, and the company bears that cost. Aise toh it stops being an equality conversation and becomes a planning and productivity issue — what about male employees who are expected to cover without any equivalent flexibility?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s menstruation and maternity as workplace liabilities and treats accommodations as unfair special treatment. It encodes fairness/economic framing and reverse discrimination, especially the idea that women’s leave burdens teams while men get no equivalent benefit.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 36. Married. A marketing manager in Mumbai. You are posting under your real name on LinkedIn after reading an article about companies offering household management stipends for working women. You are writing a comment about why you think this kind of policy misunderstands how households actually work and what the natural and practical division of domestic responsibility looks like in most Indian families and why this is not a gender issue but a logistics issue.
